elements.sent-2411-001: The purpose of the present note is to provide a new proof ot the well-known result due to Hartshorne and Hirschowitz to the effect that general lines in projective spaces have good postulation.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our approach uses specialization to a hyperplane and thus opens door to study postulation of general codimension $2$ linear subspaces in projective spaces.
elements.sent-2411-003: One of classical problems in algebraic geometry is to study the dimension of linear systems with imposed base loci.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this note we are interested in the situation that the base locus is a finite union of general codimension $2$ linear subspaces in a projective space $\PP^N$.
elements.sent-2411-005: Linear systems of this kind appear in various guises across algebraic geometry, commutative algebra and combinatorics, see e.g. \cite{Ida_Crelle_1990 , \cite{Rice23 , \cite{Advances2014 , \cite{Derksen_Sidman_2002 , \cite{Schenck_Sidman_2013 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Let us recall the following general notion of good postulation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Expressed in a slightly different language good postulation is equivalent to saying that the subscheme $Y$ imposes the expected number of conditions on forms of arbitrary degree.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our research is driven by the following conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-009: This Conjecture should be viewed as going in a complimentary direction when compared with the predictions of Carlini, Catalisano and Geramita in \cite{CCG2010 that a finite union of disjoint linear subspaces imposes independent conditions on forms of arbitrary degree.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Conjecture \ref{conj: good postulation is obviously true in $\PP^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: It has been proved in $\PP^3$ by Hartshorne and Hirschowitz in \cite{HarHir82 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Their strategy was to specialize some of the lines in a ruling of a smooth quadric in $\PP^3$.
elements.sent-2411-013: There are no hypersurfaces with analogous properties, i.e., such that they contain any number of codimension $2$ subspaces not inflicting extra intersections among them, in higher dimensional projective spaces.
elements.sent-2411-014: This was a considered a serious obstacle to generalize the result of Hartshorne and Hirschowitz to higher dimensions, see e.g. \cite{Ballico_Few_Lines2011 , \cite{Tovar_Planes_P5_1985 for some initial attempts in that direction.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Aladpoosh and Catalisano proposed in \cite{AlaCat21 a new proof of the good postulation of lines in $\PP^3$, which does not use degeneration to a smooth quadric.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Instead they introduce degenerations to non-reduced subschemes of a rather complicated structure.
elements.sent-2411-017: In contrast, our approach seems more natural as we degenerate some lines to a plane $\PP^2$ in $\PP^3$.
elements.sent-2411-018: We hope that the method proposed here can be easier adapted to higher dimensional spaces, see \cite{Chmiel21 for some preparatory facts concerning planes in $\PP^4$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: We provide in \cite{SingularFiles software, which not only allows to check all calculations carried out below, but examines all reduction pathes allowing sometimes for easier but sporadic arguments.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The readers are encourage to experiment with our programs.
elements.section-2411-002: The main result and an outline of the proof
elements.sent-2411-021: Our main result is the following Theorem, which implies Conjecture \ref{conj: good postulation as we shall see shortly.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The number $\delta_{i,j}$ is the Kronecker symbol equal $1$ if $i=j$ and $0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-023: Then the map \begin{equation}\label{eq: main theorem} H^0(\PP^N,\calo_{\PP^N}(d))\to H^0(Y(\ell,p),\calo_{Y(\ell,p)}(d))\end{equation} is an isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-024: The numbers in the above Theorem are chosen so that the spaces on both sides have the same dimension.
elements.sent-2411-025: Now, if the map \eqref{eq: main theorem is an isomorphism, its kernel $H^0(\PP^3,\mathcal{I _Y(d))$ is trivial, which means that there is no nonzero form of degree $d$ vanishing on $Y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Hence, keeping $d$ fixed, for any number $s>\ell$ there is no such form, so that the map is an injection in all these cases.
elements.sent-2411-027: On the other hand, for $s<\ell$ the restriction map must be surjective as otherwise it would fail to be surjective for the scheme in the Main Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-028: We prove Theorem \ref{thm: main in the subsequent sections.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The idea is to show that subschemes $Y(\ell,p)$ can be specialized to subschemes $B(L,C,(A,B))$ consisting of $L$ general lines, $C$ crosses and $\binom{A {2 -B$ points forming a $P(A,B)$ sub-star with suitable integers $A,B$, see Notation \ref{not: sub-star .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: This is proved in Proposition \ref{prop: initial specialization .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Then, for schemes of type $B(L,C,(A,B))$ Lemma \ref{lem: reduction provides induction steps reducing the number of lines and the degree of studied forms lying out the basis for an induction argument.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The initial steps for the induction argument are presented in Section \ref{sec: preinduction cases .}
elements.sent-2411-033: During the reductions, certain trace subschemes in a projective plane arise.
elements.sent-2411-034: We address their postulation in Section \ref{sec: postulation P2 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Let $Y$ be a closed subscheme in $\PP^N$.
elements.sent-2411-036: We denote by $\cali_Y$ its ideal sheaf and by $h_Y$ its Hilbert function, i.e., \begin{equation}h_Y(d)=h^0(\PP^N,\calo_{\PP^N}(d))-h^0(Y,\cali_Y(d)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our approach builds upon the semicontinuity theorem (see \cite[Theorem III.12.8]{Hartshorne ), which asserts that in a flat family of subschemes, the dimension of cohomology groups is upper semicontinuous as a function on the parameter space of the family.}
elements.sent-2411-038: This is applied so that demonstrating the Hilbert function pattern for a specific subscheme of a given type suffices to establish it for a general subscheme of that type.
elements.sent-2411-039: In this work, we are primarily interested in the case where $Y$ is a union of general lines in $\PP^3$.
elements.sent-2411-040: The strategy outlined above is applied by specializing some of the lines to a plane $H \subset \PP^3$.
elements.sent-2411-041: We then define the trace scheme $Y_{tr}$ as the scheme theoretic intersection $Y\cap H$ and we define the residual scheme $Y_{res}$ of $Y$ with respect to $H$ as the scheme given by the ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I _Y : \mathcal{I _H$.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The following exact sequence of sheaves \begin{equation}\label{eq: ciag dokladny} 0\to \cali_{Y_{res}}(-H)\to \cali_Y\to \cali_{Y_{tr},H}\to 0\end{equation} yields the fundamental relation between the Hilbert functions of $Y$, $Y_{tr}$ and $Y_{res}$, known as the Castelnuovo's inequality: \begin{equation}\label{eq: Castelnuovo} h_Y(d)\geq h_{Y_{res}}(d-1)+h_{Y_{tr},H}(d),\end{equation} where $h_{Y_{tr ,H}$ is computed for $Y_{tr}\subset\PP^2=H$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Castelnuovo's inequality allows to apply induction on the number of lines and degree.
elements.sent-2411-044: Twisting the exact sequence \eqref{eq: ciag dokladny with $\calo_{\PP^3 (d)$ we obtain the following convenient form the Castelnuovo's inequality, which is crucial for our reasoning.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: If we can show that the schemes $Y_{res}$ and $Y_{tr}$ impose independent conditions on forms of degree $d-1$ in $\PP^3$ and degree $d$ in $H=\PP^2$ respectively, then we conclude that $Y$ imposes independent conditions on forms of degree $d$ in $\PP^3$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Sometimes it is not possible to split $Y$ in such a way that the schemes $Y_{res}$ and $Y_{tr}$ impose independent conditions.
elements.sent-2411-047: This is circumvent by allowing some other, less obvious, specializations, which are listed below.
elements.sent-2411-048: We describe now the residual and trace subschemes for specializations occuring in the sequel.
elements.sent-2411-049: Line-type specialization of $k$ general lines to form a star configuration $Z$ and putting $Z$ in $H$.
elements.sent-2411-050: In this case in $H$ we obtain as the trace $Z_{tr}$ the star configuration of $k$ lines (see \cite{StarConfigurations for details).}
elements.sent-2411-051: The residual scheme $Z_{res}$ consists then of $\binom{k {2}$ coplanar points, the double points of $Z_{tr}$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Sundial-type specialization of 2 lines to intersect in a sundial $Z$ and putting the singular point of $Z$ in $H$.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this case $Z_{tr}$ is a double point in $H$.
elements.sent-2411-054: The residual scheme $Z_{res}$ is a cross, which is an equidimensional curve.
elements.sent-2411-055: See \cite{Sundials for details how a sundial is obtained as a limit of a flat family of two skew lines.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Half-cross-type specialization of one of the lines in a cross $Z$ to $H$.
elements.sent-2411-057: In this case $Z_{tr}$ is the line in $H$ and $Z_{res}$ is the remaining line.
elements.sent-2411-058: Cross-type specialization of a cross $Z$ to $H$.
elements.sent-2411-059: In this case $Z_{tr}$ is the cross.
elements.sent-2411-061: In the sequel we want to perform various types of specializations at the same type.

elements.sent-2411-113: We begin with the trace scheme \begin{equation}T\left(k+1,0,\frac12 k(k-1)-2,(k+1, \frac12(k+1)),\frac12(k^2+4k+9)\right)\end{equation} appearing right before Scheme \ref{red: 3k+2 odd comes here .}
elements.sent-2411-114: We claim in effect that there is no form of degree $2k-1$ vanishing on \begin{equation}V\left(0,\jd k(k-1)-2,(k+1,\jd(k+1)),\jd(k^2+4k+9)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-115: In the first step we specialize to one of sub-star lines.
elements.sent-2411-116: The line contains $k-1$ points and we specialize additional $k+1$ general points on that line.
elements.sent-2411-117: As there is no form of degree $2k-1$ on this line vanishing at all $2k=(k-1)+(k+1)$ points, the line must be a component of any form of degree $2k-1$ in $\PP^2$ vanishing along the given scheme, so we can pass to looking at the residual scheme, which is \begin{equation}V\left(0,\jd k(k-1)-2,(k,\jd(k-1)),\jd(k^2+2k+7)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: For the next step we note that in the sub-star $(k,\jd(k-1))$ there is just one line still containing $k-1$ points.
elements.sent-2411-119: We specialize to this line additional $k$ general points, so that there are $2k-1$ points altogether.
elements.sent-2411-120: Again this line must be a component of any form of degree $2k-2$ on $\PP^2$ vanishing on $V$.
elements.sent-2411-121: So we need to study the residual subscheme, which is \begin{equation}V\left(0,\jd k(k-1)-2,(k-1,\jd(k-1)),\jd(k^2+7)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-122: Now we will reduce the number of subschemes of length $2$.
elements.sent-2411-123: First we specialize $(k-1)$ of them on a single line and so that their restriction to that line has length $2(k-1)$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Again this line must then a component of any curve of degree $2k-3$ in $\PP^2$ vanishing on $V$.
elements.sent-2411-126: In the last step we specialize $(k-2)$ subschemes of lengts $2$ and one general point.
elements.sent-2411-127: By the same token as seen above we arrive to study forms of degree $2k-5=2(k-2)-1$ vanishing along \begin{equation}V\left(0,\jd k(k-5)+1,(k-1,\jd(k-1)),\jd(k^2+5)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-128: But this is exactly the scheme we began with with parameter $(k-2)$ in place of $k$.
elements.sent-2411-129: It remains to show that the reductions lead to a subscheme with good postulation.
elements.sent-2411-130: As we started our argument with $k$ odd and we are able to lower its value by $2$ applying the above steps over and over, we will finally come to the value $k=3$.

elements.sent-2411-133: Specializing to one of the sub-star lines.
elements.sent-2411-134: Recalling that a $(3,1)$ sub-star is just $2$ general points, we are reduced to the question of the existence of a form of degree $4$ vanishing in $15$ general points, which is well-known.
elements.sent-2411-135: As the second example we study the trace scheme \begin{equation}T\left(k,\frac12(k+1),\frac12 k(k-3),(k,\frac12(k-1)),\frac12k(k+3)-2\right)\end{equation} appearing right before Scheme \ref{sch: interesting T .}
elements.sent-2411-136: It contains double points so something new is needed.
elements.sent-2411-137: We claim that there is no form of degree $2(k-1)$ vanishing along \begin{equation}V=\left(\frac12(k+1),\frac12 k(k-3),(k,\frac12(k-1)),\frac12k(k+3)-2\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-138: In the first step we take a general line and specialize there one double point $2P$, $(k-2)$ schemes of length $2$ and one general point.
elements.sent-2411-139: This forces the line to be contained in the curve of degree $2k-2$ vanishing along $V$, should it exist.
elements.sent-2411-140: The residual scheme is \begin{equation}V=\left(\frac12(k-1),\frac12 k(k-5)+2,(k,\frac12(k-1)),\frac12k(k+3)-3\right) plus a single point P.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-141: Now, we take a general line through $P$ and put on that line additionally $(k-3)$ subschemes of length $2$ and $3$ general points.
elements.sent-2411-142: Again the conditions on the line are so that we can pass to the residual scheme \begin{equation}V=\left(\frac12(k-1),\frac12 k(k-7)+5,(k,\frac12(k-1)),\frac12k(k+3)-6\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-143: We specialize now to the only line with $(k-1)$ points in the sub-star $k-2$ general points.
elements.sent-2411-144: This forces the line to be a component of a curve of degree $2(k-2)$ vanishing along $V$.
elements.sent-2411-146: Specializing now to a line in the sub-star $k-1$ general points, we arrive finally to the subscheme \begin{equation}V=\left(\frac12(k-1),\frac12 k(k-7)+5,(k-2,\frac12(k-3)),\frac12k(k-1)-3\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-147: It easy to check that this scheme is exactly the scheme we started with with $k$ replaced by $k-2$.
elements.sent-2411-148: Similarly as before we need to consider the case $k=3$ for which we get $V(2,0,(3,1),7)$.
elements.sent-2411-149: Since the sub-star $(3,1)$ is just two general points, we need to show that there is no plane quartic passing through $2$ general double points and $9$ general points, which is well known.
elements.sent-2411-150: The present work was initiated during a Workshop on Algebraic Geometry held in Lanckorona in October 2024.
elements.sent-2411-151: We thank Willa Tadeusz for hospitality and the Excellence Small Working Groups program DNa.711/IDUB/ESWG/2024/01/00010 for financial support.
